column2: Reducing the size of a file so it uses less storage space and can be transmitted faster (using less bandwidth).
Reducing file size with no permanent loss of data; the original file can be reconstructed exactly when decompressed.
Reducing file size by permanently removing some data (often data the eye/ear is less sensitive to); the original cannot be reconstructed exactly.

column1: Give a typical use of lossless compression.
column2: Text files, executable programs, spreadsheets, ZIP, PNG — anywhere detail cannot be lost.

column1: Give a typical use of lossy compression.
column2: Images (JPEG), audio (MP3) and video, where small quality loss is acceptable for much smaller files.
A lossless technique that replaces runs (sequences of repeated identical values) with a single value and a count of how many times it repeats.
Most effective with long runs of repeated data (e.g. simple images); it can increase file size when data has few or no repeats.
3W2B4W (value followed by run length, lossless).
A lossless technique that stores recurring patterns/words once in a dictionary and replaces them in the file with a short reference (e.g. an index number).

column1: Why must a dictionary be sent with dictionary-compressed data?
column2: Because the references in the file have no meaning without the dictionary; it is needed to reconstruct the original data.
Scrambling data into unreadable ciphertext using an algorithm and a key, so only someone with the correct key can decrypt it back to plaintext.
Encryption that uses the same single key to both encrypt and decrypt the data.

column1: What is the key distribution problem?
column2: In symmetric encryption the shared key must be exchanged securely; if it is intercepted during exchange the encryption is broken.
Encryption using a pair of mathematically related keys: a public key (shared openly) and a private key (kept secret).

column1: How do public and private keys relate in asymmetric encryption?
column2: Data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the matching private key.

column1: Give one advantage of asymmetric over symmetric encryption.
column2: It solves the key distribution problem because the public key can be shared openly without compromising security.

column1: Give one disadvantage of asymmetric encryption.
column2: It is slower and more computationally expensive than symmetric encryption.
Applying a hash function to an input to produce a fixed-length output (a hash/digest); it is one-way and deterministic.

column1: What does it mean that hashing is 'one-way'?
column2: You cannot feasibly reverse a hash back to the original input.
When two different inputs produce the same hash value/output.
Storing passwords securely (store the hash, not the password) and hash tables for fast (close to O(1)) lookup.

column1: Why store password hashes rather than passwords?
column2: If the database is stolen, attackers do not get the actual passwords; an entered password is hashed and compared to the stored hash.
Encryption is reversible with a key; hashing is one-way and not designed to be reversed.

column1: What is a flat file database?
column2: A database held in a single table/file; it suffers from data redundancy and update/inconsistency anomalies.
A database where data is split across multiple linked tables (using keys), reducing redundancy and improving consistency and scalability.
Entity = a category of thing data is stored about (a table); attribute/field = a property/column; record/row/tuple = all data about one instance.
An attribute (or set of attributes) that uniquely identifies each record in a table.
An attribute in one table that is the primary key in another table, used to link the two tables.
A primary key made up of two or more attributes combined, because no single attribute is unique on its own.
An attribute used to allow records to be searched/indexed quickly even though it is not the primary key.

column1: How is a many-to-many relationship resolved in a relational database?
column2: By introducing a linking (junction) table, typically with a composite key, creating two one-to-many relationships.
The process of organising data in a database to reduce redundancy and remove anomalies while keeping data consistent.
No repeating groups; each field contains a single (atomic) value; there is a primary key.
In 1NF and every non-key attribute depends on the whole primary key (remove partial dependencies — relevant with a composite key).
In 2NF and no non-key (transitive) dependencies — no non-key attribute depends on another non-key attribute.
Every non-key attribute depends on 'the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key'.
Ensuring a foreign key value always refers to a valid existing primary key in the related table (or is null), preventing orphaned records.
A single logical operation made up of one or more steps that must all succeed or all fail together.

column1: What do the letters ACID stand for?
column2: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — properties of a reliable transaction.
Atomicity: the transaction is all-or-nothing; if any part fails it is rolled back. Durability: once committed it is permanently saved, even after a failure.
Consistency: a transaction takes the database from one valid state to another without breaking rules. Isolation: concurrent transactions do not interfere, as if run one after another.
Locking a record while one transaction updates it so others cannot edit it at the same time, preventing the lost update problem (risk: deadlock).

column1: Name SQL keywords to retrieve and order data.
column2: SELECT (columns), FROM (table), WHERE (filter rows), ORDER BY ... ASC/DESC (sort).

column1: What does JOIN ... ON do in SQL?
column2: Combines columns from related tables where a matching key value links them.

column1: Write SQL to add a record to Student (StudentID 5, Surname 'Lee').
column2: INSERT INTO Student (StudentID, Surname) VALUES (5, 'Lee');

column1: What is the difference between a LAN and a WAN?
column2: A LAN covers a small area (one site, organisation-owned cabling); a WAN covers a large area using third-party infrastructure (the internet is the largest WAN).
Star: all devices connect to a central node (fails if node fails). Mesh: devices interconnected with multiple paths, very resilient but expensive when wired.
Client-server: servers provide resources to clients (central control, single point of failure). P2P: devices have equal status and share directly (cheaper, harder to manage).
Data is split into packets that are routed independently (possibly different routes) and reassembled in order at the destination; efficient and resilient.
A dedicated physical path is reserved between sender and receiver for the whole communication; guarantees bandwidth but wastes capacity when idle.
The Domain Name System: servers that translate human-readable domain names into the IP addresses computers use to locate each other.

column1: Name the four layers of the TCP/IP stack.
column2: Application, Transport, Network/Internet, Link/Data link.

column1: What does the Transport layer do?
column2: Splits data into packets, manages end-to-end delivery, error checking and reassembly, and adds port numbers (TCP/UDP).

column1: What does the Network/Internet layer do?
column2: Adds source and destination IP addresses and routes packets across networks (IP).

column1: What is the difference between a switch and a router?
column2: A switch connects devices within a LAN using MAC addresses; a router connects different networks and routes packets using IP addresses.
A unique 48-bit physical hardware address assigned to a NIC, used at the link layer to identify devices on a local network.
HTTP transfers web pages between server and browser; HTTPS is HTTP encrypted with TLS/SSL for secure web communication.

column1: Purpose of SMTP, POP3 and IMAP?
column2: SMTP sends email; POP3 retrieves email (usually downloads and removes it from the server); IMAP retrieves email keeping it on the server, synced across devices.
Hardware/software that monitors and controls traffic entering/leaving a network against rules (packet filtering) to block unauthorised access.
An intermediary between clients and the internet that can hide users' IP addresses, cache pages and filter/log requests.

column1: What do HTML, CSS and JavaScript each do?
column2: HTML defines structure/content; CSS defines presentation/style; JavaScript adds interactivity/behaviour (usually client-side).
An algorithm that ranks web pages by importance, mainly based on the number and importance of links pointing to them.

column1: How does PageRank treat links from important vs many-linking pages?
column2: A link from a high-PageRank page is worth more; a page linking to many pages spreads its vote thinly (rank divided by number of outgoing links).

column1: What is client-side processing? Give a drawback.
column2: Code runs in the user's browser (e.g. JavaScript); fast feedback but can be viewed, disabled or bypassed, so it is not secure on its own.

column1: What is server-side processing? Give a drawback.
column2: Code runs on the web server (e.g. PHP, database queries); secure and hidden but increases server load and adds network round-trip delay.

column1: Application: why must payment validation be done server-side too?
column2: Client-side validation can be bypassed or disabled by the user, so security-critical checks must also be validated/processed server-side.

column1: Application: which compression for a legal document, and why?
column2: Lossless — no data may be permanently lost, since corrupting a byte could change the document's meaning.
